text: The surgeon frees the small bowel mesentery to maximize its length, preparing for the assessment of the pouch's microvascularization. In cases requiring additional length for anastomosis, the surgeon clamps the vascular supply of the pouch using a vascular clamp.

The screen displays three views: normal light, infrared light, and enhanced reality. The black and white infrared view confirms that the vascular supply to the bottom end of the pouch is preserved, even after the elongation procedure.

The surgeon divides the vessels, allowing the pouch to be safely positioned at the bottom end for anastomosis. After completing the anastomosis, the intra-abdominal view shows the joint at the top end. Upon injecting endocyanin green, the microvessels are visibly highlighted, indicating successful vascularization up to the anastomosis site.

The surgeon performs an endoanal view of the G-pouch, inspecting the interior for any signs of bleeding and assessing the vascularization. Once confirmed to be well vascularized, the procedure can proceed to the ileostomy.

The surgeon inspects the stable line of the G-pouch for any signs of bleeding. If bleeding is observed, it is localized and stitched to ensure hemostasis. Once confirmed that there is no bleeding, the operation can be safely concluded.
